verses.commentary: Return from Egypt. Abram comes back to the land of promise, wealthier but morally compromised. Lot remains with him, setting up the coming separation.
The wealth from Egypt is emphasized. Material blessing accompanies Abram, though gained through questionable means. God's promise includes prosperity even when human faithfulness falters.
Abram retraces his steps to Bethel, the place of the altar. This is a return to worship and recommitment, back to where his spiritual journey began in the land.
Spiritual renewal. Abram returns to worship. Calling on the LORD's name suggests repentance and renewed relationship. Back to the place of the altar means back to the place of devotion.
Lot has prospered too. Both men have significant possessions. This sets up the coming conflict where blessing creates practical problems.
Resource scarcity. Blessing becomes a problem. Too much wealth for one area. Separation becomes necessary, showing how material prosperity can strain even family relationships.

Conflict emerges. Not between Abram and Lot directly but between their workers. The presence of Canaanites makes the situation worse: internal strife before external enemies is shameful and undermines their witness.
Abram takes initiative as the elder. He values relationship over rights. The emphasis on kinship shows peace is prioritized over possessions. Mature faith seeks reconciliation.

Abram offers Lot first choice. This is remarkable generosity. As the elder and the one with God's promise, Abram has rights to the land. Yet he defers. Faith trusts God's provision rather than grasping at entitlements.

Lot chooses by sight. The comparison to Eden and Egypt is ominous. The parenthetical note foreshadows judgment. What looks like Eden will become like fire and brimstone. Appearances deceive; spiritual realities matter more.

Lot chose for himself. Selfishness. Eastward continues the pattern of moving away from God's presence (3:24, 4:16, 11:2). Separation is complete. Choices based on self-interest lead away from blessing.
Abram stays in Canaan, the land of promise. Lot gravitates toward the cities. Near Sodom will soon become in Sodom (14:12). Lot's choice leads toward disaster, showing incremental compromise.
Editorial comment. Sodom is morally corrupt. Lot chooses well-watered land but wicked neighbors. Material prosperity doesn't guarantee spiritual safety. The narrator warns what Lot doesn't yet see.
After Lot's departure, God speaks. Timing matters. God renews the promise once Abram has acted in faith and generosity. Divine confirmation follows human obedience.

God confirms the promise with visual confirmation. Forever (olam) suggests permanence. The promise is not just for Abram's lifetime but for all generations. What Abram gave up to Lot, God multiplies back.
Innumerable descendants. Like dust suggests both abundance and earthly, humble origin. An impossible promise for a childless, elderly couple. Faith must believe the impossible.
Active participation. Walk the land. Claim it by traversing it. Faith involves action. God gives, but Abram must walk. Promises require human response.
Abram settles at Hebron. Another altar. Worship marks his journey. Hebron becomes a significant location in Israel's history, later the burial place of the patriarchs.
